# Macronutrients: carbohydrate, protein, fat and fibre

> *The energy-yielding nutrients — but the recurring lesson is that **quality**, not just
> quantity, drives health. Same kilocalories can mean very different physiology.*

## The macronutrients at a glance

Macronutrients are the nutrients we need in **gram quantities** and the ones that supply
**energy**. There are three — carbohydrate, protein and fat — plus **dietary fibre**, a
carbohydrate we mostly *don't* digest but which earns its own place. Their energy yields:

| Macronutrient | Energy (kcal/g) | Headline role |
|---|---|---|
| Carbohydrate | ~4 | Primary, glucose-yielding fuel |
| Protein | ~4 | Structure, enzymes, immune & transport functions |
| Fat | ~9 | Energy store, cell membranes, essential fatty acids |
| Fibre | ~2 | Fermented by gut microbes; not a fuel in the usual sense |
| *(Alcohol)* | *~7* | *Not a nutrient — empty energy, shown for contrast* |

Fat is the most energy-dense, which is why fat-rich foods raise total intake quickly. But
energy density is only part of the story. The question that matters clinically is almost
never *"how many grams?"* — it is *"what kind, from what food?"*

::: {.callout-important}
## The one idea to carry out of this chapter
"Are carbs bad? Is fat bad?" are **malformed questions**. The health effect of a
macronutrient depends on its **form and food source**, not a single good/bad label. Hold
this thought — we will return to it at the end.
:::

**How we'll take each one.** For carbohydrate, fat and protein we ask the same four
questions — *what is it, what does it do, what happens to it in the body,* and *how do we
choose and cook it well?* Watch for three recurring boxes that turn the science into
practice: 🔬 **Food science** (the chemistry), 🍜 **Eating out** (choices in Singapore's
food environment), and 🍳 **Healthy kitchen** (cooking at home).

## Carbohydrate

**What it is.** Carbohydrates run a wide spectrum, and where a food sits on it matters far
more than the word "carb":

> **free sugars / refined starches  →  intact whole grains + fibre**

- **Free sugars** — added sugars plus those in syrups and fruit juices — are the
  carbohydrate to *limit*. The WHO caps them at **<10% of energy**, with further benefit
  below 5% [@who2020diet]. (The natural sugar inside *whole* fruit is **not** a free sugar
  and is not the target.)
- **Refined starches** (white rice, white bread) are digested fast and stripped of fibre.
- **Whole grains and fibre** sit at the protective end.

![Whatever their source, digestible carbohydrates are broken down to simple sugars — chiefly **glucose**, shown here in its ring form.](/figures/structures/beta-d-glucose.svg){#fig-glucose fig-alt="Skeletal ring structure of beta-D-glucose." width="230"}

**What it does.** Carbohydrate is the body's main everyday fuel. Glucose powers the brain
(which normally depends on it almost exclusively) and fuels working muscle; the surplus is
stored as **glycogen** in liver and muscle, and adequate carbohydrate "spares" protein from
being burned for energy.

**From plate to cell.** Starch digestion begins in the mouth (salivary amylase) and resumes
in the small intestine (pancreatic amylase), where **brush-border** enzymes (maltase,
sucrase, lactase) finish the job into single sugars. Glucose and galactose are absorbed
through the gut lining via the **SGLT1** co-transporter (fructose via GLUT5) and travel to
the liver in the portal blood, where **insulin** drives their uptake and storage. *(Digestion
in detail → the Digestion & Absorption chapter; the fed/fasted handling of glucose → the
Integrative Metabolism chapter.)*

**Quality: not all carbohydrate is equal.** In a major series of meta-analyses, higher fibre
and whole-grain intake was associated with roughly **15–30% lower** all-cause and
cardiovascular mortality and lower rates of coronary disease, stroke, type 2 diabetes and
colorectal cancer — with benefit strengthening up to about **25–29 g of fibre a day**
[@reynolds2019carb].

::: {.callout-warning}
## How strong is the fibre evidence?
Mostly **prospective cohort** studies plus some trials — so the certainty is *moderate at
best* for fibre, and lower for whole grains and for glycaemic index/load. These are
**associations**, not proof of cause. Teach the benefit; keep the caveat attached.
:::

### Glycaemic index and glycaemic load

Not all carbohydrate raises blood glucose at the same speed. The **glycaemic index (GI)**
ranks a food by how much a portion containing **50 g of available carbohydrate** raises
blood glucose over about two hours, relative to pure glucose (set at **100**)
[@atkinson2021gitables]. Lower-GI foods give a gentler, slower rise:

- **Low GI:** ≤55 — most legumes, intact whole grains, most whole fruit, milk
- **Medium GI:** 56–69 — basmati rice, sweet potato
- **High GI:** ≥70 — white rice, white bread, glucose (= 100)

But GI has a blind spot: it fixes the carbohydrate at 50 g — a portion you may never
actually eat. **Glycaemic load (GL)** corrects for this by scaling GI to the *real* serving:

$$ \text{GL} = \frac{\text{GI} \times \text{available carbohydrate (g) per serving}}{100} $$

graded **low ≤10, medium 11–19, high ≥20** per serving. GL therefore captures *both* the
**quality** of the carbohydrate (its GI) and the **quantity** actually eaten.

::: {.callout-tip}
## The watermelon trick
Watermelon has a **high GI (~76)** — yet a normal slice holds only ~6 g of carbohydrate, so
its **GL is ~5 (low)**. A small portion of a high-GI food can be a low-load choice. The
reverse also holds: a *low*-GI noodle like *bee hoon* becomes a **high**-GL plate at hawker
portions. So "high GI = avoid" is the wrong shortcut — **both quality and portion matter.**
:::

Explore the relationship below. Pick a food — its GI sets the **height** of the glucose
curve — then drag the portion, which grows the **shaded area** (the glycaemic load). Switch
on **Compare** to see the watermelon-versus-potato paradox: near-identical peaks, very
different areas.

<div data-island="gi" data-src="/diagrams/glycemic-index-load.yml"></div>

A few honest caveats keep GI in proportion: it is **not** a measure of healthiness
(chocolate is low-GI); a food has **no single fixed GI** (ripeness, processing, cooking and
the rest of the meal all shift it); and the evidence linking GI/GL to hard outcomes is the
*weakest* strand of carbohydrate-quality research — **low to very low certainty**, well
below fibre and whole grains [@reynolds2019carb]. For most patients the practical message
is not a GI number but the familiar one: favour **whole, intact, fibre-rich** carbohydrates
in **sensible portions** — the wholegrain quarter of My Healthy Plate [@hpbHealthyPlate].

![Brown rice keeps its bran and germ — more fibre and a gentler glucose rise than white rice. Photo: Ceeseven, CC BY-SA 4.0.](/figures/food/brown-rice.jpg){#fig-brown-rice fig-alt="A bowl of cooked brown rice." width="320" .lightbox}

### From science to plate

::: {.callout-note}
## 🔬 Food science: what cooking does to starch
Cooking **gelatinises** starch — granules swell and become easier to digest, so more-cooked,
finely-milled starch raises glucose faster [@cai2021starchstructure]. Cook a starchy food
and then **chill it**, and some starch *retrogrades* into **resistant starch**, which
escapes digestion and behaves partly like fibre. Real, but modest — a refinement of "quality
over quantity," not a way to make white rice "low-carb."
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out
Choose the **brown-rice or wholemeal** option where offered; ask for **less rice, more
vegetables**; a *cai png* plate works as "**2 vegetables + 1 protein, less gravy**"; and pick
a **Nutri-Grade A/B** drink or plain water over a Grade-C/D one [@hpbHealthyPlate;
@hpbNutriGrade].
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍳 Healthy kitchen
Cook pasta and noodles **al dente**; try the **cook–cool–(reheat)** trick for rice and
potato to raise resistant starch [@sonia2015rice]; and choose **intact whole grains**
(brown/red rice, rolled oats) over products made from finely-milled flour.
:::

## Fat

**What it is.** For decades the advice was "eat less fat." The better message is "eat
**better** fat." Rank fats by their relationship to cardiovascular risk:

> **trans fat  →  saturated  →  monounsaturated  →  polyunsaturated (incl. omega-3)**

- ***Trans* fat** is the clearest harm. WHO recommends **<1% of energy** and elimination of
  industrial sources [@who2020diet].
- **Saturated fat** raises LDL cholesterol; the practical move is to **replace it with
  unsaturated fat** — not with refined carbohydrate.
- **Mono- and polyunsaturated fats** (olive oil, nuts, fish) are the ones to favour;
  omega-3 and omega-6 are *essential* fatty acids the body cannot make.

![Nuts are a whole-food source of mono- and polyunsaturated fats. Photo: HaJunkiyada, CC BY-SA 4.0.](/figures/food/mixed-nuts.jpg){#fig-nuts fig-alt="An assortment of mixed nuts." width="320" .lightbox}

**What it does.** Fat is the body's most concentrated energy store, a structural component of
every **cell membrane**, the carrier for the **fat-soluble vitamins** (A, D, E, K), and the
source of the **essential fatty acids** (omega-3, omega-6) the body cannot make.

**From plate to cell.** Fat has to be coaxed into mixing with water: **bile salts** from the
liver emulsify it into tiny droplets, and pancreatic **lipase** frees fatty acids and
monoglycerides that ferry across the gut lining in **micelles**. Inside the intestinal cells
they are re-assembled into triglycerides and packaged into **chylomicrons**, which enter the
**lymphatics** (not the portal vein) before reaching the blood. *(Bile and micelle handling
→ the Digestion & Absorption chapter; lipoprotein transport and fat metabolism → the
Integrative Metabolism chapter.)*

**Quality: type matters more than total.**

::: {.callout-note}
## "Low fat" can backfire
Chasing "low fat" often means swapping fat for refined starch or sugar — frequently a
*worse* trade. The target is fat **quality**, and the replacement nutrient matters. *(Where
saturated fat sits within a whole food — dairy versus processed meat — is an area of genuine
ongoing scientific debate; the first-year message stays simple: replace with unsaturated and
whole foods.)*
:::

### Seeing the difference: *cis*, *trans* and saturated

Oleic acid (olive oil) and elaidic acid (the classic industrial *trans* fat) contain the
**same atoms** — they differ only in the geometry of one double bond. Start with the **2D
formula**, then rotate the **3D** model, and add **stearic acid** (a saturated fat) for
comparison. A single **cis** bond kinks the chain, keeping unsaturated fats liquid; the
**trans** bond leaves the chain almost straight, so — like fully saturated stearic acid — it
packs tightly and behaves like a solid fat. The bend from one cis bond is modest;
polyunsaturated fats, with several cis bonds, are far more kinked. The shape *is* the
explanation.

<div data-island="molecule" data-src="/structures/dietary-fats.mol.yml"></div>

### From science to plate

::: {.callout-note}
## 🔬 Food science: geometry and oxidation
The *cis* kink you just rotated is why olive oil is liquid and *trans*/saturated fats are
more solid — and why *trans* fat behaves like saturated fat in the body. Heat matters too:
overheating oil past its smoke point, or **reusing frying oil** repeatedly, oxidises it into
polar compounds and aldehydes — and the more polyunsaturated the oil, the faster it happens.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out
Prefer **steamed, grilled or stir-fried** over deep-fried; ask for **less oil**; **skip the
chicken skin**; and choose **fish** as a protein when you can. Be aware that some stalls
**reuse frying oil**, which Singapore's Food Agency flags as a quality problem
[@sfaReusingOils].
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍳 Healthy kitchen
Cook with **unsaturated oils** and don't heat them until they smoke; **don't reuse frying
oil** again and again; and reach for **steaming, boiling or a light stir-fry** over
deep-frying — fried foods draw in oil and energy, and frequent intake tracks with higher
type 2 diabetes and heart-failure risk [@gadiraju2015fried].
:::

## Protein

**What it is.** Protein is built from ~20 **amino acids**, several of which are **essential**
(they must come from the diet). A protein's **quality** reflects how well its amino acids
match human needs: animal proteins are typically "complete," while most single plant
proteins are lower in one or more essential amino acids — easily fixed by combining plant
foods across the day.

![L-alanine, a representative amino acid: an amino group (–NH₂), a carboxyl group (–COOH), and a variable side chain (here a methyl) that distinguishes one amino acid from another.](/figures/structures/l-alanine.svg){#fig-alanine fig-alt="Skeletal structure of the amino acid L-alanine." width="230"}

**What it does.** Protein is the body's building and working material — **structure** (muscle,
collagen, skin), **enzymes**, antibodies, and transport and signalling molecules. Unlike
carbohydrate and fat, it is not primarily an energy store: the body keeps only a small
circulating **amino-acid pool** and turns its proteins over continuously.

**From plate to cell.** Protein digestion starts in the **stomach**, where acid unfolds it
and **pepsin** begins cleaving it; pancreatic proteases (trypsin, chymotrypsin) and
brush-border peptidases then break it down to **amino acids** and short peptides, absorbed
into the portal blood. Cells use these to build their own proteins; surplus amino acids are
**deaminated** (the nitrogen leaving as urea) and their carbon skeletons burned for energy or
turned into glucose. *(Digestion in detail → the Digestion & Absorption chapter; amino-acid
metabolism and nitrogen balance → the Integrative Metabolism chapter.)*

**Quality: adequacy and completeness.** A useful population reference is about **0.8 g/kg/day**
— adequate for most healthy adults, though needs rise in older age, illness and athletic
training. Plant proteins arrive as a **package deal** with fibre and unsaturated fat, and
protein has the highest **thermic effect** of the macronutrients, contributing modestly to
energy expenditure and satiety.

![Tofu — a plant protein that also brings unsaturated fat, with no cholesterol. Photo: yearofeats, CC BY 2.0.](/figures/food/tofu.jpg){#fig-tofu fig-alt="Cubes of fresh tofu." width="320" .lightbox}

### From science to plate

::: {.callout-note}
## 🔬 Food science: heat, browning and protein
Heat **denatures** protein — unfolding it so enzymes can reach it — which usually makes it
*easier* to digest: cooked egg protein is absorbed far better than raw (~91% vs ~51%)
[@evenepoel1998egg]. But the high-heat **Maillard** browning that adds flavour has a
downside: in starchy foods it forms **acrylamide**, a genotoxic carcinogen of concern
[@efsa2015acrylamide]; in charred meat it forms other mutagens, though the human cancer
evidence is inconclusive [@nciCookedMeats].
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out
Favour **lean or plant proteins** — fish, *tofu*/*tau kwa*, *dhal*, eggs, skinless chicken —
over fatty or **processed meats**, and go easy on heavily **charred** satay or barbecue.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍳 Healthy kitchen
Aim for **golden, not brown** when frying or roasting, and **don't blacken** food
[@fsaGoForGold]; gentle methods (poaching, steaming, braising) cook protein well without the
high-heat by-products.
:::

## Fibre

**What it is.** Dietary fibre is carbohydrate the small intestine cannot digest —
**soluble** (viscous; oats, legumes, some fruit) and **insoluble** (bulking; wholegrain
bran, vegetable skins), much of it **fermentable** by gut bacteria.

**What it does.** Fibre slows digestion and the glucose rise, adds bulk and aids
regularity, and — the fermentable part — feeds gut microbes that make **short-chain fatty
acids**. Higher fibre intake is among the best-supported dietary associations with lower
disease risk (the Carbohydrate evidence above) [@reynolds2019carb]. *(How microbes ferment
fibre into short-chain fatty acids → the Gut Microbiome chapter.)*

**From science to plate.** The practical message is one line: **choose whole, intact plant
foods** — wholegrains, legumes, fruit and vegetables — which bring their fibre with them,
rather than refined foods with the fibre stripped out.

## From nutrients to patterns

Modern guidelines have moved decisively from "hit this macronutrient ratio" to **"eat these
foods, in this pattern"** [@aha2021diet]. There is **no single ideal macronutrient split**;
guidelines deliberately avoid prescribing one and advise individualising — and referring to
a dietitian where needed [@ada2024care]. The consistent themes are: favour whole grains,
plants and unsaturated fats; minimise added sugar, *trans* fat, sodium and ultra-processed
foods.

## The Singapore picture

You have met Singapore's everyday tools per nutrient — **My Healthy Plate** ("quarter,
quarter, half": ¼ wholegrains, ¼ protein, ½ fruit and vegetables) [@hpbHealthyPlate] and
**Nutri-Grade** A–D drink labels [@hpbNutriGrade]. The population picture shows why they
matter: the National Nutrition Survey 2022 found only about **4% of staples** eaten are
wholegrain (against a ~30% recommendation), saturated fat makes up a large share of fat
intake, and most residents exceed the sodium target [@mohNNS2022]. The gap between what the
science recommends and what a nation actually eats is exactly where a brown-rice swap, a
less-oily order, and a plainer drink earn their keep.

## So — are carbs bad?

Put it together. The question dissolves once you ask *which carbohydrate, from what food?*
A bowl of sugary drink and white rice, and a bowl of brown rice with vegetables and fish,
are both "carbs" — and they are not remotely the same for health. Your first-year job is to
give **quality** guidance ("more wholegrains, fewer free sugars, better fat"), anchor it to
My Healthy Plate, and **refer to a dietitian** when a patient wants a restrictive or
therapeutic diet [@ada2024care]. Resist prescribing grams; that comes later [@crowley2019].

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/macronutrients.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: "Doctor, are carbs bad?"

A patient has cut out all rice and bread. Help them replace a slogan with a useful
distinction. Your choices branch the case.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/are-carbs-bad.case.yml"></div>
